data.title: Sequential versus Integrated Optimization: Production, Location, Inventory Control and Distribution
data.summary: This work should be read as a core integrated-supply-chain optimization reference rather than an AI paper. In the most thesis-relevant chapter, it compares sequential (siloed) and integrated optimization for a multi-product, multi-plant, multi-period, multi-echelon supply chain with production, inventory, direct-shipment distribution, dynamic distribution-center location, and delivery-time windows. The paper formulates the integrated problem as a MILP, studies exact CPLEX performance on realistic instances, and introduces an ALNS-plus-exact matheuristic to handle the large binary decision space. Its main value for the thesis is to justify why integrated MILP modeling matters and why computational acceleration is needed.
key_findings: Problem scope: integrated production, location, inventory, and distribution planning in a multi-product, multi-plant, multi-period, multi-echelon supply chain with direct shipment and delivery-time windows.
Modeling contribution: provides a concrete MILP formulation for integrated supply-chain planning with binary setup and facility-location decisions plus downstream flow and inventory decisions.

Computational finding: exact CPLEX solves only the smallest instances to optimality; on larger instances, optimality gaps become substantial within a one-hour limit, which is direct evidence that realistic integrated MILPs are hard to solve exactly.
Managerial finding: sequential planning is materially worse than integrated planning, so the paper strongly supports the thesis argument against siloed supply-chain decision making.

Methodological finding: the ALNS-plus-exact matheuristic is a relevant predecessor pattern for thesis methods that combine heuristic search over hard binary decisions with exact optimization of the remaining subproblem.

Literature-review value: the broader Darvish 2017 thesis also contains a useful integrated-supply-chain literature classification, so this source is not only a single experiment paper but part of a larger methodological reference set.
data.methodology: The thesis-relevant core is a MILP-based integrated production-location-inventory-distribution model with direct shipment and delivery-time windows. The study compares multiple sequential planning procedures against a fully integrated formulation, then solves the integrated model using both exact branch-and-bound with CPLEX and an ALNS-based matheuristic that searches difficult binary design and setup decisions while repeatedly solving the remaining flow subproblem exactly.
data.relevance_notes: High relevance for the graduation thesis. This is one of the strongest references for the modeling legitimacy of integrated supply-chain MILP, because it directly shows why production, location, inventory, and distribution should be optimized jointly instead of sequentially. It is also useful as a methodological bridge: not because it proves ML works, but because it documents an exact-plus-matheuristic decomposition pattern and the computational intractability that motivates later learning-assisted acceleration. In writing, it should support the integrated-model rationale, the complexity argument, and the design logic of heuristic-plus-exact hybrid solving. It should not be presented as an ML, ANN, or LLM paper.
data.source_excerpt: Integrated production, location, inventory, and distribution problem. The objective is to operate a production-distribution system that minimizes production, location, inventory, and distribution costs while satisfying demands within a predetermined delivery time window.
